chunk_summary: Guidelines for selecting combined oral contraceptives (COCs) in adolescents with PCOS, focusing on thrombosis risk assessment, safe prescribing practices, and follow-up care.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of thrombosis and cardiovascular risk screening before prescribing COCs in PCOS patients.
Identify contraindications to estrogen-containing contraceptives, including uncontrolled hypertension and thrombophilia.
Learn to select low-dose estrogen formulations and progestins with favorable androgenic profiles for PCOS management.
Recognize the need for follow-up to monitor side effects, bleeding patterns, and thrombotic symptoms.
key_facts: Obesity increases baseline venous thromboembolism risk in PCOS patients.
Low-dose ethinyl estradiol (20–35 micrograms) is recommended to minimize thrombosis risk.
Contraindications to COCs include uncontrolled hypertension, severe liver disease, and known thrombophilia.
COCs do not treat insulin resistance or weight trajectory; lifestyle therapy and metformin remain essential.
Follow-up should include monitoring for thrombotic symptoms like leg swelling, chest pain, and sudden dyspnea.
reference_quote: Obesity increases baseline venous thromboembolism risk, so documentation of eligibility and counseling should be as deliberate as the metabolic work-up. (Source: Monash University Evidence-Based Guidelines 2023)
document_key_topics: Lifestyle therapy and metabolic management in PCOS
